content.problemStatement:
Zonal Computing Olympiad 2013, 10 Nov 2012
Hobbes has challenged Calvin to display his chewing skills and
chew two different types of Chewing Magazine's Diabolic
Jawlockers chewing gum at the same time. Being a generous sort of
tiger, Hobbes allows Calvin to pick the two types of gum he will
chew.


Each type of chewing gum has a hardness quotient, given by a
non-negative integer.  If Calvin chews two pieces of gum at the
same time, the total hardness quotient is the sum of the
individual hardness quotients of the two pieces of gum.


Calvin knows that he cannot chew any gum combination whose
hardness quotient is K or more. He is given a list with
the hardness quotient of each type of gum in the Diabolic
Jawlockers collection.  How many different pairs of chewing gum
can Calvin choose from so that the total hardness quotient
remains strictly below his hardness limit K?


For instance, suppose there are 7 types of chewing gum as
follows:







 Chewing gum type  1  2  3  4  5  6  7  Hardness quotient  10  1  3  1  5  5  0 


 If Calvin's hardness limit is 4, there are 4 possible pairs he can choose: type 2 and 7 (1+0 < 4), type 3 and 7 (3+0 < 4), type 2 and 4 (1+1 < 4) and type 4 and 7 (1+0 < 4).
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines:
- The first line contains two space‑separated integers N and K, where N is the number of gum types and K is Calvin's hardness limit.
- The second line contains N space‑separated non‑negative integers, the hardness quotients of the N gum types.
All integers satisfy the constraints given below.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one non‑negative integer: the number of distinct unordered pairs of gum types whose total hardness is strictly less than K.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The four valid unordered pairs are (type2, type7), (type3, type7), (type2, type4) and (type4, type7). Each sum is strictly less than 4.
All C(5,2)=10 possible pairs have sums from 3 to 9, all below 10.
The only pair sums to 0, which is not strictly less than K=0.

solution.approach:
For each test case we need the number of unordered pairs (i, j) with i < j and a[i] + a[j] < K.
A naïve O(N²) scan is too slow for N up to 100 000. The optimal method is:
1. Sort the hardness array in non‑decreasing order – O(N log N).
2. Use two pointers: left starts at 0, right at N‑1.
   - If a[left] + a[right] < K, then for this left all elements from left+1 … right form valid pairs because the array is sorted. Add (right‑left) to the answer and increment left.
   - Otherwise the sum is ≥ K, so any pair involving a[right] is too large; decrement right.
3. Continue until left >= right.
The algorithm runs in O(N log N) time (dominated by sorting) and O(1) extra space besides the input array.
Edge cases handled automatically: K = 0 (no pair qualifies), duplicate hardness values, and N = 2.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (sorting dominates).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the array (in‑place sorting uses O(1) additional space).
